stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x + ([[B]]) \), which is continuous (hence Riemann integrable) on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). Partition \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \) into \( [[n]] \) equal subintervals, each of width \( [[w]] \), and let \( x_k = [[p]] + k[[w]] \) for \( k = 1, \dots, [[n]] \) be the right endpoints. What is the right-endpoint Riemann sum \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{[[n]]} f(x_k)\cdot [[w]] \)?
solution: The right-endpoint Riemann sum is \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{[[n]]} f([[p]]+k[[w]])\cdot[[w]] = \sum_{k=1}^{[[n]]} \big([[A]]([[p]]+k[[w]])+([[B]])\big)[[w]] = [[answer]] \), computed directly from the sum.
distractor_rationales: This uses the LEFT endpoints \( x_{k-1} \) instead of the right endpoints \( x_k \).
This uses the midpoint rule (evaluating \( f \) at the interval's center) instead of a right-endpoint sum.
This evaluates \( f \) only at the left endpoint \( p \) and multiplies by the full interval width, skipping the partition.
This evaluates \( f \) only at the right endpoint \( q \) and multiplies by the full interval width, skipping the partition.
This is off by \( w \) from the correct Riemann sum.
This is off by \( w \) from the correct Riemann sum.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x + ([[B]]) \), which is continuous (hence Riemann integrable) on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). Partition \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \) into \( [[n]] \) equal subintervals, each of width \( [[w]] \), and let \( x_{k-1} = [[p]] + (k-1)[[w]] \) for \( k = 1, \dots, [[n]] \) be the left endpoints. What is the left-endpoint Riemann sum \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{[[n]]} f(x_{k-1})\cdot [[w]] \)?
solution: The left-endpoint Riemann sum is \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{[[n]]} f([[p]]+(k-1)[[w]])\cdot[[w]] = \sum_{k=1}^{[[n]]} \big([[A]]([[p]]+(k-1)[[w]])+([[B]])\big)[[w]] = [[answer]] \), computed directly from the sum.
distractor_rationales: This uses the RIGHT endpoints \( x_k \) instead of the left endpoints \( x_{k-1} \).
This uses the midpoint rule (evaluating \( f \) at the interval's center) instead of a left-endpoint sum.
This evaluates \( f \) only at the left endpoint \( p \) and multiplies by the full interval width, skipping the partition.
This evaluates \( f \) only at the right endpoint \( q \) and multiplies by the full interval width, skipping the partition.
This is off by \( w \) from the correct Riemann sum.
This is off by \( w \) from the correct Riemann sum.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[A]]x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \), which is continuous on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \) and hence Riemann integrable: its Riemann sums over ever-finer partitions of \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \) converge to a single value \( \displaystyle\int_{[[p]]}^{[[q]]} f(x)\,dx \) regardless of the partition or sample points chosen. What is this common limiting value?
solution: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the common limit of the Riemann sums is \( \displaystyle\int_{[[p]]}^{[[q]]} \big([[A]]x^{2}+([[B]])x+([[C]])\big)\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats \( f \) as if it depended only on \( q-p \), plugging the interval width directly into \( f \).
This approximates with a single left-endpoint rectangle over the whole interval instead of integrating.
This approximates with a single right-endpoint rectangle over the whole interval instead of integrating.
This reports \( f'([[p]]) \), the derivative at the left endpoint, not the integral.
This is off by two from the correct integral.
This is off by two from the correct integral.
